enrichment.short_description: Visual social media scheduling and marketing platform with Linkin.bio, best-time-to-post tools, and multi-network calendar management.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Later began life in 2014 as Latergramme, a simple tool built to help Instagram users plan and schedule their photo posts in advance. As Instagram matured and other visual-first platforms grew, the product rebranded to Later and expanded well beyond a single-network scheduler into a broader social media marketing platform. Today it serves creators, small businesses, agencies, and marketing teams who need to plan, publish, and analyze content across several social channels at once.

At its core, Later is built around a visual content calendar. Instead of a plain list of scheduled posts, users see a drag-and-drop grid that mirrors how a feed will actually look once published, which was an early differentiator for brands that care about visual consistency on Instagram and Pinterest. That visual-first philosophy has stayed central to the product even as it has added support for TikTok, Facebook, LinkedIn, YouTube, Threads, Snapchat, and X-style short-form content workflows.

Later also owns Linkin.bio, one of the more established link-in-bio tools on the market, which turns a single Instagram or TikTok bio link into a clickable, shoppable landing page. Combined with scheduling, basic analytics, and a media library, Later positions itself less as a single-purpose scheduler and more as a lightweight hub for planning, publishing, and measuring visual social content, particularly for teams whose main channels are Instagram, Pinterest, and TikTok. The company went through an ownership change when it was acquired by influencer marketing platform Mavrck in 2022, and the two brands were folded into a single unified business a couple of years later, though Later's core product identity as a visual scheduler and Linkin.bio provider has remained largely consistent throughout that transition.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Later Is For
paragraphs: Later is best suited to solo creators, influencers, and small business owners who post primarily visual content and want an easy way to plan a feed in advance without a steep learning curve. Its drag-and-drop calendar and preview grid make it approachable for people who are not full-time social media managers but still want their Instagram or TikTok presence to look intentional.

It also fits marketing teams at small-to-midsize brands and e-commerce businesses that lean heavily on Instagram, Pinterest, and TikTok for discovery and traffic, especially when the Linkin.bio landing page is used to drive shoppers from a bio link to product pages. Boutique social media agencies managing a handful of client accounts often choose Later for its approval workflows and multiple social set options on the higher-tier plans.

Because pricing scales by the number of connected social profiles rather than purely by seats, Later can also work well for freelancers and consultants juggling several client accounts under one roof, provided the total profile count stays within a plan's allowance.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Large enterprises with complex, high-volume publishing needs across many brands, and teams that require deep listening, sentiment analysis, or advanced social CRM capabilities, will likely find Later's feature set comparatively lean next to enterprise suites built specifically for those workflows.

Teams whose primary channels are text-first or B2B-heavy, such as organizations centered on LinkedIn thought leadership or X-native conversation rather than visual feeds, may not benefit as much from Later's visual-calendar-first design, since that strength is most pronounced for image and video-led platforms.

Budget-conscious users managing many social profiles should also be cautious, since Later's paid tiers price around a limited number of connected profiles and additional 'social sets,' which can push costs up quickly for anyone managing accounts for numerous clients or brands on a single plan.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: Later's feature set centers on a visual, drag-and-drop content calendar that lets users plan posts across Instagram, Facebook, TikTok, Pinterest, LinkedIn, YouTube, Threads, and Snapchat from one dashboard. Auto-publishing is available for most supported networks, reducing the need to manually push content live at scheduled times.

Beyond scheduling, the platform includes a media library for organizing images and video assets, hashtag suggestion tools, Instagram best-time-to-post recommendations based on an account's own engagement history, and first-comment scheduling so hashtags or calls to action can be added automatically after a post goes live. Linkin.bio extends the platform into landing-page territory, letting a single bio link route visitors to a tappable, shoppable grid of recent posts and products.

Team and agency features include content approval workflows, user roles and permissions, and multiple 'social sets' for separating client or brand accounts on higher-tier plans, along with analytics and reporting to track engagement and link performance over time.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Later Experience
paragraphs: Day to day, using Later feels closer to arranging a mood board than filling out a scheduling form. New users typically start by connecting their social profiles, then drag media into the calendar view to see how a week or month of posts will look before anything actually publishes. This visual-first workflow is one of the more consistently appreciated aspects of the product among long-time users.

The mobile app mirrors much of the desktop experience, which matters for creators and social media managers who capture content on the go and want to caption, tag, and schedule from a phone rather than waiting to sit down at a laptop. Notifications and a straightforward posting queue help keep occasional users from having to relearn the interface each time they log back in.

The learning curve is generally shallow for core scheduling and calendar tasks, though newer users sometimes need a moment to find their footing with Linkin.bio customization or the analytics dashboards, since those areas carry more configuration options than the core publishing flow. Overall, most reviews describe the day-to-day experience as clean, visually oriented, and quick to pick up for anyone already comfortable with Instagram or Pinterest.

features.title: Visual content calendar
features.description: Drag-and-drop calendar that previews how a scheduled feed will actually look, built specifically around visual platforms like Instagram and Pinterest.

features.title: Multi-network scheduling
features.description: Plan and auto-publish posts across Instagram, Facebook, TikTok, Pinterest, LinkedIn, YouTube, Threads, and Snapchat from a single dashboard.

features.title: Linkin.bio
features.description: A customizable, shoppable link-in-bio landing page that turns a single Instagram or TikTok bio link into a clickable grid of posts and products.

features.title: Best-time-to-post recommendations
features.description: Suggests optimal posting times for Instagram based on an account's own historical engagement data.

features.title: First-comment scheduling
features.description: Automatically publishes a pre-written first comment, such as hashtags or a call to action, right after a post goes live.

features.title: Media library
features.description: Centralized storage for images and videos so content can be organized and reused across multiple scheduled posts.

features.title: Approval workflows
features.description: Lets teams and agencies route content through review and sign-off before it is scheduled or published, available on higher-tier plans.

features.title: Analytics and reporting
features.description: Tracks post and link performance, engagement trends, and Linkin.bio click-through data to inform future content decisions.

features.title: Multiple social sets
features.description: Allows agencies and multi-brand teams to organize and manage separate groups of connected social profiles within one account.

features.title: Mobile app
features.description: iOS and Android apps for scheduling, captioning, and reviewing the content calendar from a phone or tablet.

pros: Visual, drag-and-drop calendar makes feed planning intuitive, especially for Instagram and Pinterest
Linkin.bio is a mature, well-integrated link-in-bio tool bundled with scheduling
Approachable learning curve for solo creators and small teams new to social scheduling
Supports a wide range of networks including TikTok, Pinterest, and Threads alongside the mainstays
Mobile app allows content creators to plan and schedule directly from a phone
cons: Paid plans are priced around a limited number of connected social profiles, which can get costly for agencies with many clients
Analytics and reporting are less advanced than dedicated social analytics or enterprise suites
Feature set leans heavily toward visual platforms, offering less specialized value for text-first or B2B-focused channels
Some advanced features, like extra social sets or added users, require upgrading to a higher tier
Free plan is limited enough that most regular users will need a paid tier fairly quickly

faqs.question: What is Later used for?
faqs.answer: Later is a social media scheduling and marketing platform used to plan, schedule, and analyze posts across networks like Instagram, TikTok, Pinterest, Facebook, LinkedIn, YouTube, Threads, and Snapchat, along with managing a Linkin.bio landing page.

faqs.question: Does Later have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, Later offers a limited free plan aimed at individuals with basic scheduling needs, in addition to paid tiers that unlock more profiles, users, and features. Paid plans also include a free trial period.

faqs.question: How much does Later cost?
faqs.answer: Later's paid plans start at around $25 per month for the Starter tier, with Growth and Scale tiers priced higher for teams and agencies that need more social profiles, collaboration tools, and advanced features. Annual billing typically offers a discount over monthly pricing.

faqs.question: Is Later good for TikTok and Pinterest, not just Instagram?
faqs.answer: Yes, while Later started as an Instagram-only tool, it has expanded to support TikTok, Pinterest, Facebook, LinkedIn, YouTube, Threads, and Snapchat, though its visual calendar and best-time-to-post features remain especially strong for Instagram and Pinterest.

faqs.question: What is Linkin.bio and is it included with Later?
faqs.answer: Linkin.bio is Later's built-in link-in-bio tool that turns a single bio link into a clickable, shoppable landing page tied to recent posts. It is included as part of Later's platform rather than sold as a fully separate product.

use_cases.title: Planning a cohesive Instagram feed
use_cases.description: A creator or brand drags upcoming photos and captions into the visual calendar to preview how the grid will look before anything is scheduled or published.

use_cases.title: Driving shoppable traffic from a bio link
use_cases.description: An e-commerce or DTC brand uses Linkin.bio to route Instagram and TikTok visitors from a single bio link to a landing page of tappable product posts.

use_cases.title: Managing multiple client accounts
use_cases.description: A boutique social media agency organizes several client brands into separate social sets, routing content through approval workflows before it goes live.

use_cases.title: Coordinating a small marketing team's content calendar
use_cases.description: A marketing team collaborates on a shared visual calendar across Instagram, Facebook, and Pinterest, using analytics to see which posts and links perform best.

features: Limited number of connected social profiles
Access to Linkin.bio in a limited form
Additional connected social profiles and users
Highest number of connected profiles and social sets
Priority support for larger teams and agencies

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Later is a visual social media scheduling platform with a drag-and-drop calendar, Linkin.bio, and multi-network publishing for creators and brands.

enrichment.short_description: Lattice is a people management platform for performance reviews, goals/OKRs, engagement surveys, and career development.
enrichment.starting_price: Custom pricing (third-party sources report modular per-seat pricing starting around $4-$11/seat/month, billed annually)

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Lattice is a people management platform founded in 2015 and headquartered in San Francisco. It was built on the premise that annual performance reviews alone don't give managers or employees enough visibility into how work is actually going, so the product combines performance management, goal tracking, engagement measurement, and career development into one connected system rather than a set of disconnected point tools.

The platform is organized around a handful of core modules that companies can adopt together or separately: performance reviews and 360-degree feedback, goals and OKRs, employee engagement and pulse surveys, 1:1 meeting management, career development frameworks, compensation planning, and a core HRIS layer for storing employee records and org data. More recently, Lattice has layered AI capabilities on top of these modules to help HR teams and managers move faster on writing reviews, summarizing survey results, and tracking goal progress.

Because Lattice sells its products in a modular, quote-based way rather than through simple self-serve tiers, it tends to be positioned as a mid-market to enterprise people platform rather than a lightweight tool for very small teams. Buyers typically work with a Lattice sales team to configure which modules and seat counts fit their organization before signing an annual contract.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Lattice Is For
paragraphs: Lattice is best suited to HR and People Operations teams at growing or established companies that want to formalize performance management without stitching together several disconnected tools. Organizations that already run structured review cycles, OKR programs, or engagement surveys - or want to start doing so consistently - tend to get the most value from consolidating that work in one place.

It also fits companies that care about connecting performance data to career growth and compensation decisions, since Lattice's modules are designed to share data across review cycles, goal tracking, and merit or compensation planning. People analytics leaders who want dashboards and benchmarking across engagement, performance, and turnover trends are a natural audience as well.

Managers and employees who want a lighter-weight layer for 1:1s, feedback, and praise - not just a once-a-year review form - are also a good fit, since much of Lattice's day-to-day usage happens outside of formal review windows through ongoing feedback, recognition, and meeting tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Very small companies or early-stage startups without a dedicated HR function may find Lattice more platform than they currently need, especially given its sales-led, quote-based pricing and annual contract structure rather than simple self-serve monthly plans.

Teams that only need one narrow capability - for example, just pulse surveys, or just OKR tracking - may find a single-purpose tool cheaper and faster to set up than adopting Lattice's broader modular suite, particularly if they don't plan to use performance reviews, career development, or compensation planning together.

Organizations that want fully transparent, published pricing they can evaluate without a sales conversation may also find the process friction-heavy, since exact costs depend on which modules, add-ons, and seat counts are negotiated with Lattice's sales team.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At its core, Lattice offers structured performance reviews with customizable cycles, competency-based questions, and 360-degree feedback, alongside goals and OKR tracking that lets companies cascade objectives from company-wide priorities down to individual contributors and monitor progress over time.

Employee engagement is handled through pulse and eNPS surveys with benchmarking and reporting, while 1:1 meeting tools give managers and employees shared agendas, notes, and follow-up items. Career development features include competency matrices, career tracks, and individual growth plans, and a compensation management module supports merit cycles and pay-band planning.

Underneath these modules sits a core HRIS for centralized employee records, org charts, and workforce reporting, plus integrations with common payroll, calendar, and communication tools. Lattice has also added AI features, including an AI agent for answering HR questions, AI-assisted review writing and survey analysis, and tools for summarizing goal progress.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Lattice Experience
paragraphs: Day to day, using Lattice generally means logging into a web dashboard (or the iOS/Android mobile app) to check active goals, prep for an upcoming 1:1, give a teammate praise, or fill out a section of an in-progress review cycle. The interface is widely described as clean and approachable for non-HR employees, not just administrators.

For HR and People Ops admins, the experience centers on configuring review cycles, survey schedules, and goal structures, then monitoring completion and results through dashboards. Because performance, engagement, and goals data live in the same system, admins can look at trends - such as how engagement scores relate to turnover or goal completion - without exporting data between separate tools.

Implementation typically involves working with Lattice's team to decide which modules to turn on, migrate employee data into the HRIS layer, and configure review templates and permissions, which suits organizations willing to invest some setup time in exchange for a more unified long-term system.

features.title: Performance Reviews
features.description: Customizable review cycles with 360-degree feedback and competency-based questions for structured performance evaluation.

features.title: Goals & OKRs
features.description: Cascade company, team, and individual goals so progress and alignment can be tracked over time.

features.title: Employee Engagement Surveys
features.description: Pulse and eNPS surveys with benchmarking and heatmaps to measure and track workforce sentiment.

features.title: 1:1 Meetings
features.description: Shared agendas, notes, and talking points that help managers and direct reports run consistent check-ins.

features.title: Career Development
features.description: Career tracks, competency matrices, and individual development plans that connect growth to performance data.

features.title: Compensation Management
features.description: Merit cycles and pay-band planning tools that tie compensation decisions to performance and goal data.

features.title: Core HRIS
features.description: Centralized employee records, org charts, and workforce reporting as the data backbone for other modules.

features.title: Lattice AI
features.description: AI-assisted review writing, survey analysis, goal summarization, and an AI agent for answering common HR questions.

features.title: People Analytics
features.description: Dashboards that surface trends across performance, engagement, and turnover to inform people decisions.

features.title: Integrations
features.description: Native and API-based connections to common HRIS, payroll, calendar, and communication tools.

pros: Combines performance reviews, goals, engagement surveys, and career development in one connected platform
Widely regarded as intuitive and easy to use for both managers and employees, not just HR admins
AI-assisted features help speed up writing reviews and summarizing survey and goal data
Modular product structure lets companies choose only the modules relevant to their needs
Broad integration ecosystem with common HRIS, payroll, and workplace communication tools
cons: Pricing is not published transparently and requires a sales conversation to get an exact quote
Annual, quote-based contracts can make it less accessible for very small or self-serve buyers
Some capabilities, such as AI features and compensation planning, may require add-on modules at extra cost
Configuring review cycles, goal structures, and permissions can take real setup time for larger organizations
Sales-led buying process may feel heavier than lightweight, single-purpose HR point tools

faqs.question: What is Lattice used for?
faqs.answer: Lattice is a people management platform used for performance reviews, goal and OKR tracking, employee engagement surveys, 1:1 meetings, career development, and compensation planning.

faqs.question: How much does Lattice cost?
faqs.answer: Lattice does not publish full self-serve pricing; it sells modular products on a per-seat, annually billed basis, with exact costs depending on which modules and seat count a company selects, quoted through its sales team.

faqs.question: Does Lattice offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Lattice is primarily a sales-led platform aimed at HR teams, and it typically offers guided demos rather than a broadly advertised self-serve free trial; prospective customers should contact Lattice directly to confirm current options.

faqs.question: Does Lattice have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: Yes, Lattice offers mobile apps for iOS and Android that let employees and managers handle 1:1s, feedback, praise, and surveys from their phone.

faqs.question: What size companies use Lattice?
faqs.answer: Lattice is used by companies ranging from growing startups to large enterprises, with its modular product and pricing structure allowing organizations to scale usage as their HR needs grow.

use_cases.title: Performance Review Cycles
use_cases.description: HR teams run structured, recurring performance reviews with 360-degree feedback, competency questions, and calibration across the organization.

use_cases.title: OKR and Goal Alignment
use_cases.description: Leadership sets company-wide objectives and cascades them into team and individual goals that are tracked for progress throughout the year.

use_cases.title: Employee Engagement Measurement
use_cases.description: People analytics teams deploy pulse and eNPS surveys and use benchmarking data to monitor and improve workforce sentiment over time.

use_cases.title: Manager 1:1s and Career Development
use_cases.description: Managers and employees use shared meeting agendas, feedback, and individual development plans to support ongoing career growth.

features: Foundation layer for other Lattice modules
Core admin permissions and data management
